abstract: Hydrogen fuel cell commercialization is currently limited by the high cost of Pt-based cathode catalysts required to drive the sluggish oxygen reduction reaction (ORR). Here, we report an atomically dispersed dual-metal catalyst (Zn/Co-N-C) derived from a bimetallic metal-organic framework (MOF) consisting of Co-N4 and Zn-N4 local architectures. The optimized Zn/Co-N-C catalyst exhibits exceptional ORR activity in alkaline media with a half-wave potential of 0.938 V vs. RHE and outstanding durability, losing only 8.5 mV after 50,000 electrochemical cycles. When integrated into an anion exchange membrane fuel cell (AEMFC), the catalyst achieves a record peak power density of ~1 W cm⁻² for single-atom catalysts at an ultra-low cobalt loading. To elucidate the catalytic mechanism, operando X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) was employed, revealing that the Co-N4 moieties serve as the primary active centers. The spectroscopic data, characterized by potential-dependent edge shifts and coordination changes, indicate the formation of an Oads-Co-N4 intermediate during the reaction. These experimental findings are supported by a potential-dependent microkinetic model and core-level shift calculations, which confirm the thermodynamic favorability of the Co-N4 site over Zn-N4 and provide atomistic detail on the bonding of oxygenated intermediates. This study offers a comprehensive understanding of the dynamic evolution of active sites in dual-metal single-atom catalysts and establishes a framework for the rational design of non-precious metal electrocatalysts for high-performance energy conversion.
